source.title: High-Level Prediction Signals in a Low-Level Area of the Macaque Face-Processing Hierarchy

confidence_rationale: Provides direct empirical evidence that lower cortical areas receive typed prediction signals from higher areas that carry view-invariant, identity-specific properties -- a signature of typed-object passing across hierarchy levels rather than raw sensory signal passing. This is the physiological correlate of ARC-022's typed-object abstraction principle. However, mapping fidelity is moderate: the face-processing hierarchy (ML -> AL areas) is a specialised visual object domain, and the inference to E1->E2 typed-object passing requires treating low-level prediction-error responses as the functional equivalent of E1 consuming E2-derived objects. The finding that statistical learning enables lower areas to test predictions at higher abstraction levels supports ARC-022's claim that each layer 'operates on the output type of the layer below without re-instantiating lower-level computations'.
failure_signatures: The study is restricted to face processing in macaque inferotemporal cortex -- a highly specialised domain with a well-characterised object hierarchy. Generalisation to action/planning hierarchies requires substantial inference.

The effect depends on prior statistical learning of face sequences; whether analogous typed-object prediction signals exist in motor/planning hierarchies without extensive sequence training is not shown.
The study supports feedback prediction signals carrying higher-level object properties but does not directly test the pipeline topology (E1->E2->DMN->Goal->Hippocampus) that ARC-022 specifies.

mapping.source_claim_statement: After statistical learning of face sequences, the low-level face area ML computes deviation of actual from predicted stimuli. The prediction error signals in ML display identity specificity and view invariance characteristic of higher-level areas (AL), not ML's typical tuning. Lower-level areas can test predictions at greater abstraction levels than standard feedforward processing allows.
mapping.ree_translation: The result instantiates the typed-object principle at the physiological level: higher levels send back typed prediction objects (view-invariant identity representations) that constrain what lower levels compare against. This is precisely what ARC-022 claims: each layer operates on output-type objects from the layer above (in feedback) / below (in feedforward), so the comparison at E1 is not against raw sensory predictions but against E2-typed transition predictions. The view-invariance of the prediction signal is the neuroscientific analogue of z_gamma (unified latent) carrying typed object-level information rather than raw pixel-level information.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The study concerns visual object identity prediction, not action/motor prediction. The transfer to E1 (sensory-motor prediction) and E2 (transition-model objects) requires treating the face-processing cascade as a canonical example of the general cortical hierarchy principle.
mapping.source_context: Macaque electrophysiology + fMRI; face-processing cascade as model of general cortical predictive coding architecture

confidence_components.notes: Neuron is a top-tier venue; Freiwald lab is a leading group on face processing. Source quality high. Mapping fidelity penalised for domain specificity (face/object vs. action/planning). Transfer risk moderate-to-high for applying face-processing results to agency hierarchy.
